unit.name: Dropout, normalization, and training logistics recap
unit.description: Clarifies dropout test-time scaling, the normalization versus initialization discussion, and experiment tracking before the RNN lecture begins.
unit.summary: Dropout was clarified as convention-dependent: most libraries use p as probability of dropping a unit, while the slide used p as probability of keeping a unit active [ts=36s]. The key rule is to make test-time output match expected train-time output; dropping 25% of activations means scaling by 0.75 at test time [ts=52s]. The LayerNorm example used a 2D quadrant classification task and showed normalization can reduce the harm from bad initialization, but cannot replace good Kaiming-style initialization [ts=91s] [ts=125s]. Normalization can also hurt when exact coordinates matter because mean/std normalization removes some spatial information [ts=145s]. The recap also situates non-recurrent training topics: setup choices, transfer learning, training dynamics, validation tuning, and tools such as Weights & Biases [ts=180s] [ts=240s].

key_points.text: At test time, scale dropout activations so expected test output equals expected training output.
LayerNorm can help bad initialization but a performance gap remains without good initialization.
Normalization may hurt if exact spatial coordinates are required.
Weights & Biases is shown as a tool for comparing hyperparameter runs and validation performance.

kp_catalog.name: Dropout and normalization implementation caveats
kp_catalog.description: Practical details for dropout test-time scaling and when normalization helps or hurts.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because dropout and normalization implementation caveats clarifies practical details for dropout test-time scaling and when normalization helps or hurts, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
